headline: Rice-Fish Farming Cuts Disease and Boosts Crop Yields
meta_description: Scientists find that raising fish in rice paddies reduces schistosomiasis transmission while increasing crop yields by 25%, offering dual benefits for health a…
summary_short: A new farming method combining rice and fish production reduces a chronic disease affecting 220 million people while increasing harvests by 25%.
body:
Researchers from Notre Dame have discovered that an integrated farming approach—raising freshwater fish alongside rice in paddies—offers dual benefits for health and agricultural productivity. The method significantly reduces transmission of schistosomiasis, a chronic parasitic disease that affects more than 220 million people globally, with the heaviest burden falling in sub-Saharan Africa.

Schistosomiasis is transmitted through contact with infected water, and the disease causes serious long-term health complications. By introducing fish into rice paddies, the farming system disrupts the disease's transmission cycle while simultaneously boosting crop yields by approximately 25%. The research, published in Nature Sustainability, demonstrates how ecological thinking can solve interconnected problems of health and poverty.

This approach is particularly valuable for farming communities in affected regions, as it improves both household food security and health outcomes without requiring external inputs or technology. By showing that sustainable farming methods can address disease while increasing productivity, the research opens pathways for communities to improve their circumstances through innovation grounded in their own agricultural practices.
